stories.headline: French Peacekeeper Killed in Deliberate Attack in Southern Lebanon; Macron Blames Hezbollah
stories.summary:
A French peacekeeper serving with the United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon (Unifil) was killed and three others wounded Saturday after their patrol came under small-arms fire in the village of Ghanduriyah in southern Lebanon. Unifil described the attack as deliberate, stating the patrol had been clearing explosive ordnance along a road to reconnect isolated UN positions when it was ambushed at close range.

French President Emmanuel Macron placed blame squarely on Hezbollah, saying 'everything suggests that responsibility for this attack lies with Hezbollah,' and demanded Lebanese authorities immediately arrest the perpetrators. France's Armed Forces Minister Catherine Vautrin said the soldier was 'hit immediately by a direct shot from a small arms weapon' and could not be revived by fellow soldiers.

Hezbollah denied any connection to the incident, calling the accusations 'rushed' and 'baseless,' and urged caution pending a Lebanese army investigation. Lebanon's President Joseph Aoun condemned the attack and told Macron those responsible would be brought to justice, while Prime Minister Nawaf Salam ordered a formal investigation.

The killing raises serious tensions in southern Lebanon, where a 10-day ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon took effect on April 16. The attack follows the deaths of three Indonesian peacekeepers in late March. Unifil warned that deliberate attacks on UN personnel constitute grave violations of international humanitarian law.

stories.headline: Gunman Opens Fire and Takes Hostages in Kyiv Supermarket, Killing at Least Two
stories.summary:
A gunman opened fire and took hostages inside a supermarket in Kyiv's southern Holosiivskyi district on Saturday, killing at least two people and injuring others, according to Kyiv Mayor Vitali Klitschko. Ukrainian Interior Minister Ihor Klymenko confirmed the hostage situation and said the attacker shot at police officers before being killed by responding forces.

At least ten people were transported to hospital, including a child, though the full number of casualties remained unclear in the immediate aftermath. Kyiv police reported the incident via Telegram and coordinated the response with interior ministry forces.

The attack comes at an already heightened moment of tension in Ukraine, with the country simultaneously enduring ongoing Russian strikes and active U.S.-mediated peace negotiations. The motive for the shooting has not yet been established by authorities.

stories.headline: Three Sentenced in California 'Man in Bear Suit' Insurance Fraud Scheme
stories.summary:
Three individuals have been sentenced in California after pleading no contest to felony insurance fraud charges stemming from an elaborate scheme that used a person dressed in a bear costume to stage fake attacks on luxury vehicles. Alfiya Zuckerman, 39, Ruben Tamrazian, 26, and Vahe Muradkhanyan, 32, were each sentenced to 180 days in jail and two years of supervised probation.

The scheme involved submitting fraudulent claims on a 2010 Rolls-Royce Ghost and two high-end Mercedes vehicles, with video footage purportedly showing a bear inside the cars presented as evidence. The claims were all filed for the same date and location — January 28, 2024, in Lake Arrowhead, California — raising red flags with investigators.

Biologists at the California Department of Fish and Wildlife reviewed the footage and determined it depicted a human in a bear suit rather than an actual animal. State insurance investigators launched 'Operation Bear Claw,' executed a search warrant, and discovered a bear costume at the suspects' home. The fraud totaled $141,839 in insurance payments.

California Insurance Commissioner Ricardo Lara commented on the sentencing, saying: 'What may have looked unbelievable turned out to be exactly that — and now those responsible are being held accountable.'

stories.headline: Trump Signs Executive Order Accelerating Psychedelic Drug Research, Joined by Joe Rogan
stories.summary:
President Trump signed an executive order Saturday in the Oval Office aimed at expediting research into psychedelic compounds — particularly ibogaine — for the treatment of mental health disorders including PTSD and severe depression. Podcaster Joe Rogan, a prominent advocate for psychedelic-assisted therapy, was present for the signing alongside other officials.

The order is intended to clear regulatory pathways for clinical research into substances currently restricted under federal law but used in other countries to treat veterans and others suffering from debilitating psychiatric conditions. Ibogaine, derived from an African shrub, has shown promise in small studies for treating PTSD and addiction but remains a Schedule I controlled substance in the United States.

Supporters of the measure, including Rogan and veterans' advocacy groups, argue that conventional treatments have failed too many veterans and that psychedelic therapies represent a critical frontier in mental health care. Critics have raised concerns about safety, the lack of large-scale clinical trial data, and the pace at which the administration is pushing access ahead of full regulatory review.

The signing marks a notable moment of bipartisan-adjacent appeal, with psychedelic research having drawn interest from figures across the political spectrum. The order does not legalize psychedelics but directs relevant federal agencies to streamline the research and approval process.

stories.headline: Ukraine and Russia Trade Strikes as U.S. Extends Russian Oil Sanctions Waiver
stories.summary:
Overnight Russian attacks killed at least one civilian and wounded more than two dozen others across northern and eastern Ukraine on Saturday, local officials said. A strike on Mykolaivka in the Donetsk region killed one person, while port infrastructure in Odesa was also hit. Russia's Defense Ministry reported its forces intercepted 258 Ukrainian drones over 16 regions overnight.

Ukraine struck back with drone attacks targeting oil refineries in Russia's Samara region, as well as an oil terminal in the Leningrad region and a refinery in the Krasnodar region, with fires confirmed by Russian officials. The Ukrainian military's General Staff said the strikes hit major industrial energy infrastructure, a strategy Kyiv has repeatedly employed to pressure Moscow's war economy.

The exchange comes as the U.S. Treasury Department extended for another 30 days a sanctions waiver on Russian oil shipments, drawing sharp criticism from Ukrainian officials who argue the exemption provides Moscow with additional revenue to fund its military operations. The waiver covers deliveries loaded on tankers as of Friday.

U.S.-led peace negotiations have stalled in recent weeks. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy traveled to Germany, Norway, and Italy earlier this week to shore up air defense support, while Ukraine's foreign minister met with a NATO deputy secretary general Saturday to discuss strengthening Kyiv's defenses amid what officials described as intensifying Russian aerial campaigns.

stories.headline: Federal Judge Rejects DOJ Lawsuit Seeking Rhode Island Voter Data
stories.summary:
A federal judge in Rhode Island on Friday dismissed a Department of Justice lawsuit seeking to compel the state to turn over voter registration information, dealing a setback to the Trump administration's broader effort to obtain voter data from multiple states. U.S. District Court Judge Mary McElroy ruled that federal law does not authorize the DOJ to conduct what she called a "fishing expedition" into state voter rolls.

The ruling sided with Rhode Island's top election officials and civil rights advocates who had opposed the request. The DOJ had argued that access to voter data was necessary to verify election integrity and enforce federal election law, a position the administration has advanced in similar actions against other states.

Opponents of the administration's voter data push contend the effort is an overreach that threatens voter privacy and could have a chilling effect on voter registration. Supporters argue that federal authorities have a legitimate interest in ensuring accurate voter rolls and preventing fraud.

The decision adds to a series of legal setbacks for the administration on election-related initiatives and is likely to be cited in ongoing disputes over voter data access in other jurisdictions.

stories.headline: Car Strikes Pedestrians Outside Melbourne Comic Con, Killing One
stories.summary:
A grey Toyota mounted a kerb and drove into pedestrians on Langs Road in the Ascot Vale suburb of Melbourne on Saturday, killing one person and leaving a second with life-threatening injuries, Victoria Police said. The incident occurred just before 5:00 p.m. local time outside the Melbourne Showgrounds, which was hosting the two-day Supanova Comic Con & Gaming event. A man was arrested at the scene by an off-duty police officer who received assistance from members of the public.

Detective Inspector Craig McEvoy told local media that the circumstances surrounding the collision had not yet been established and that police would investigate all avenues, including whether drugs or alcohol were involved. It remains unclear whether the incident was intentional. An eyewitness described hearing a screech before the car mounted the kerb at erratic speed and struck multiple people, then U-turned back down the street before stalling.

Thousands of attendees had been expected at the Supanova event, which bills itself as Australia's premier pop culture convention. Celebrities including Lord of the Rings actor Andy Serkis and Harry Potter actress Bonnie Wright had been announced as guests. The area around the vehicle was cordoned off as investigators processed the scene.

stories.headline: Update: Orbán Breaks Silence After Landslide Defeat as Hungary's Magyar Moves to Consolidate Power
stories.summary:
Viktor Orbán broke a days-long silence late Thursday following his party's historic defeat in Hungary's elections, saying in a YouTube interview that the result marked 'the end of an era' and that he would bear the loss 'with dignity.' Orbán took full personal responsibility for the defeat and spoke of feeling 'pain and emptiness,' though he offered limited analysis of campaign missteps beyond delays to the Russian-designed Paks 2 nuclear power station, which is running six years behind schedule. He said he would continue to lead Fidesz if re-elected by the party but acknowledged the need for 'a complete renewal,' with a leadership meeting scheduled for April 28.

Meanwhile, incoming Prime Minister Péter Magyar and his Tisza party, which won 52 percent of the vote and 141 of 199 parliamentary seats, have moved rapidly to prepare the transfer of power. Magyar secured a pledge from President Tamás Sulyok to bring forward the formation of the new parliament to the week beginning May 4. Armed with a super-majority of more than two-thirds of seats, Magyar has signaled plans to retroactively limit prime ministerial terms to two — a measure that would foreclose any potential Orbán comeback, as Orbán has already served five terms.

Magyar also gave combative interviews to Hungarian public broadcasters that had largely ignored or attacked him for years, promising legislation to suspend their news programming until impartial editors are appointed. The vote count is set to be finalized on Saturday, including recounts in closely contested constituencies and votes cast abroad. Fidesz, which held 135 seats, has been reduced to 52.

stories.headline: Two U.S. Army Soldiers Injured in Brown Bear Attack During Alaska Training Exercise
stories.summary:
Two soldiers from the 11th Airborne Division were injured Thursday after being attacked by a brown bear during a land navigation training exercise at Joint Base Elmendorf-Richardson's Arctic Valley training area near Anchorage, Alaska. Lt. Col. Jo Nederhoed, a division spokesperson, confirmed the incident and said both soldiers were receiving appropriate medical care as of Friday morning. Their identities were being withheld pending notification of next of kin.

Alaska Department of Fish and Game officials told the Anchorage Daily News the attack appeared to be defensive in nature, likely triggered after the bear recently emerged from its den. Both soldiers were carrying bear spray and deployed it during the encounter. Regional Supervisor Cyndi Wardlow said the bear spray 'may have saved their lives.' Wildlife authorities are coordinating with the military to ensure the safety of other personnel in the area.

The 11th Airborne Division, nicknamed the 'Arctic Angels,' specializes in expeditionary and cold-weather operations. The incident is under investigation by military authorities.

stories.headline: Update: Hungary's Prime Minister-Elect Magyar Moves Quickly to Consolidate Power After Orbán's Defeat
stories.summary:
Péter Magyar and his Tisza party are wasting no time preparing for the transfer of power in Hungary following their landslide victory over Viktor Orbán last Sunday, in which they won 52% of the vote and secured 141 of 199 parliamentary seats. Magyar has secured a pledge from President Tamás Sulyok to bring forward the formation of the new parliament to the week of May 4, accelerating the timeline for a new government.

Armed with a supermajority of more than two-thirds of seats, Magyar plans to retroactively limit prime ministerial terms to two — a measure that would effectively bar Orbán, who has served five terms, from ever returning to power. He also pledged to suspend public service news programs until impartial editors are appointed, citing years of coverage he characterized as biased against his movement.

Orbán broke his silence Thursday in a YouTube interview, saying 'this is the end of an era' and accepting personal responsibility for the defeat, though he offered little analysis of specific campaign mistakes. He indicated he would continue to lead Fidesz if re-elected party leader, calling for 'complete renewal' ahead of a party congress scheduled for June.

The political transition carries significant implications for Europe, as Orbán had been a key dissident voice within the EU on issues including Ukraine aid and relations with Russia. Magyar's government is widely expected to realign Hungary more closely with Brussels, potentially unlocking €17 billion in frozen EU funds.

stories.headline: Ethiopian Music Star Teddy Afro Releases Song Condemning Government Ahead of June Elections
stories.summary:
Teddy Afro, Ethiopia's most prominent musician, released a song Thursday appearing to directly condemn the government of Prime Minister Abiy Ahmed, generating more than seven million YouTube views within 24 hours. The track, titled 'Das Tal' — meaning 'put up the tent' in English — references a traditional mourning tent and laments what the artist describes as the loss of the Ethiopian national spirit.

'The spirit of being Ethiopian is now pushed away,' the 49-year-old sings. 'Where can someone go to mourn, where do you cry? In the place that raised me, in the village where I grew up, I have become a stranger, like someone with no country.' The song's release was preceded by controversy after a preview event for journalists in Addis Ababa failed to take place under unexplained circumstances.

Teddy Afro has a long history of clashing with Ethiopian authorities. He was imprisoned for 16 months roughly two decades ago on charges he maintained were politically motivated, and his 2017 album was blocked from official release despite topping the Billboard World Albums chart. He initially embraced Abiy Ahmed when the prime minister came to power in 2018 on promises of national unity, but has grown disillusioned amid widespread ethnic violence and a devastating two-year civil war in the country's north.

The song's release comes as Ethiopia approaches a general election in June, with Abiy Ahmed's government emphasizing national unity messaging. Political expression in Ethiopia carries significant risk, making the song's viral spread and explicit criticism of the government a notable development.

stories.headline: U.S.-Israel 'Special Relationship' Fracturing as Iran War Accelerates Shift in American Public Opinion
stories.summary:
The bipartisan consensus that has underpinned decades of robust U.S. military and diplomatic support for Israel is showing deepening cracks, according to multiple sources, as the ongoing Iran war accelerates a collapse in Israel's standing among Americans. Polling shows particularly sharp declines among younger Americans, a shift that is now registering in Congress, where lawmakers who were once staunch supporters are becoming vocal critics.

Representative Jason Crow (D-CO) told Axios that a serious discussion is needed about how to 'normalize' the U.S.-Israel relationship and what changes are necessary. Pro-Israel lobbying circles are also reportedly under increasing pressure as public sentiment shifts, and the trend is appearing among likely presidential candidates as well.

Israel and its supporters argue that the country is fighting for its survival against existential threats, and that American support during wartime is both a moral obligation and a strategic necessity for regional stability. Critics, including a growing number of congressional Democrats and some Republicans, contend that Israeli military conduct and Prime Minister Netanyahu's leadership have made it increasingly difficult to defend the relationship politically.

The shift represents a potential long-term realignment in one of the most durable alliances in U.S. foreign policy. Analysts note that generational change in American politics, combined with the visible human costs of Middle East conflicts, has made unconditional support for Israel a liability rather than an asset in some electoral contexts.

stories.headline: French Film Legend Nathalie Baye Dies at 77
stories.summary:
Nathalie Baye, one of France's most celebrated and decorated actresses, died Friday at her Paris home at age 77 from Lewy body dementia, a neurodegenerative disease, her family told AFP. A four-time César Award winner — France's equivalent of the Oscars — Baye starred in approximately 80 films across a career spanning five decades.

Baye got her start in François Truffaut's 1973 comedy 'La nuit américaine' (Day for Night) fresh out of drama school and went on to become a defining figure of French cinema, winning best actress at the 1999 Venice Film Festival. She also appeared in major international productions, including Steven Spielberg's 2002 film 'Catch Me if You Can,' in which she played Leonardo DiCaprio's mother. Her final on-screen role came in the 2023 Franco-Lebanese drama 'La nuit du verre d'eau.'

French President Emmanuel Macron paid tribute on social media, writing: 'We loved Nathalie Baye so much. With her voice, her smile and her grace, she has been a constant presence in French cinema over the past few decades. She was an actress with whom we loved, dreamed and grew up.' Culture Minister Catherine Pégard described her as having 'lit up a long chapter in the history of French cinema.'

Baye is survived by her daughter, actress Laura Smet, whom she had with rock icon Johnny Hallyday. Hallyday's death in 2017 sparked national mourning in France. Baye's passing removes one of the last major figures of the golden generation of French art-house cinema.

stories.headline: Carfentanil Seizures Surge Across U.S., Raising Alarm Among Drug Enforcement Officials
stories.summary:
Carfentanil, a synthetic opioid described as 100 times more potent than fentanyl and 10,000 times more potent than morphine, is surging through the U.S. drug supply at an alarming rate. The Drug Enforcement Administration recorded 1,400 carfentanil identifications in U.S. drug seizures in 2025, compared with 145 in 2023 and just 54 in 2022 — a nearly tenfold increase in two years. DEA officials warn that a quantity smaller than a grain of salt can be lethal.

Authorities believe the resurgence is linked to a Chinese government crackdown on fentanyl precursor chemicals, which has prompted Mexican traffickers to substitute carfentanil to boost the potency of a weakened fentanyl supply. The drug is often mixed with other substances and sold to users who are unaware of its presence, dramatically increasing overdose risk even when naloxone is available.

The human toll is illustrated by cases such as Michael Nalewaja, a 36-year-old Alaskan electrician who died in November 2025 after unknowingly consuming a fentanyl-carfentanil mixture he may have believed was cocaine. His mother told the Associated Press that first responders with Narcan would not have been able to save him given the drug's extreme potency.

The surge comes even as overall drug overdose deaths in the United States have trended downward over recent years, complicating the public health picture. Law enforcement officials say carfentanil poses an especially acute risk because users have no way to identify its presence without chemical testing, and standard overdose reversal doses of naloxone may be insufficient.

stories.headline: Supreme Court Rules 8-0 That Chevron's Louisiana Coastal Lawsuit Belongs in Federal Court
stories.summary:
The U.S. Supreme Court issued a unanimous 8-0 procedural ruling Friday allowing Chevron to move a major coastal damage lawsuit from Louisiana state court to federal court. The case centers on allegations that Chevron's crude oil refining activities, conducted partly under World War II-era military contracts, caused significant damage to Louisiana's coastline. A state jury had previously ordered Chevron to pay upward of $740 million in cleanup costs.

The Court held that because the refining activities were connected to federal wartime contracts, the company was entitled to defend itself in the federal court system rather than face a plaintiff-friendly state tribunal. The decision applies to a series of similar lawsuits filed by Louisiana coastal parishes against energy companies.

Supporters of the ruling framed it as a victory for legal predictability and energy production. Former U.S. Attorney General Michael Mukasey said the decision 'provides welcome assurance to private parties who act in the interest and at the behest of federal authorities that if plaintiffs and their lawyers sue over those acts, those parties will have recourse to federal courts.' Industry groups called the ruling 'a down payment toward protecting U.S. energy dominance.'

Critics of the energy industry and environmental advocates have argued that federal court venues tend to be less receptive to large damage awards and that the ruling could make it harder for states to hold oil companies accountable for environmental harm. The decision does not resolve the underlying merits of the coastal damage claims, which will now be relitigated in federal court.

stories.headline: Madonna Surprises Coachella Crowd During Sabrina Carpenter's Headline Set
stories.summary:
Madonna made an unannounced guest appearance Friday night at the Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival, joining headliner Sabrina Carpenter on stage in Indio, California. The two performed a duet of Madonna's 1990 classic 'Vogue,' a new track from Madonna's forthcoming album, and 'Like A Prayer,' drawing a massive response from the crowd.

The appearance came exactly 20 years after Madonna performed her 'Confessions On A Dance Floor' album for the first time in America at Coachella's Sahara Tent in 2006. Earlier this week, Madonna officially confirmed the release of 'Confessions II,' a sequel to that 2005 record, adding a promotional dimension to the surprise set.

Madonna told the crowd the moment felt like 'a full circle' and described it as 'very meaningful.' She also joked that it was the first time she had performed alongside someone shorter than herself, a reference to Carpenter's stature. The collaboration capped Carpenter's second consecutive Friday headline slot at the festival, which draws more than 100,000 attendees daily.

Justin Bieber is scheduled to headline Saturday night, with Colombian pop star Karol G closing out the second weekend on Sunday.

stories.headline: Japan Introduces New Term for Days Above 40°C Following Record-Shattering 2025 Summer
stories.summary:
Japan's Meteorological Agency on Friday officially introduced 'kokushobi' — variously translated as 'cruelly hot' or 'brutally hot' day — as the new designation for days when temperatures reach 40 degrees Celsius (104 degrees Fahrenheit) or above. The term was selected through a national online survey that drew roughly 478,000 responses, with 'super extremely hot day' finishing second among 13 candidate options.

The new classification follows Japan's hottest summer on record in 2025, when average nationwide temperatures ran 2.36 degrees Celsius above the historical mean. Temperatures exceeded 40°C on nine separate days between June and August, and a new national record of 41.8°C was set in the city of Isesaki. Tokyo recorded 25 days above 35°C last summer, compared with an annual average of 4.5 such days.

Japan already maintained official terminology for days exceeding 25°C, 30°C, and 35°C, and meteorologists said the frequency of extreme heat events had made a higher-tier classification necessary. The JMA is forecasting an above-normal probability of high temperatures returning this coming summer from June through August.

Scientists attribute the intensification of heatwaves globally to human-caused climate change, primarily the burning of fossil fuels. Japan's experience in 2025 — in which the cumulative number of extremely hot days also surpassed the previous record set just one year earlier in 2024 — has elevated the political and public health urgency around heat preparedness in the country.

stories.headline: Myanmar Frees Former President Win Myint in Broad Prisoner Amnesty
stories.summary:
Myanmar's military-installed President Min Aung Hlaing issued a sweeping amnesty Friday covering more than 4,500 prisoners, including former President Win Myint, to mark the country's traditional New Year. Win Myint, a close loyalist of Aung San Suu Kyi, had been serving an eight-year sentence after being arrested on the day of the February 2021 military coup.

Notably absent from the amnesty was Suu Kyi herself, who continues to serve a 27-year sentence at an undisclosed location in the capital Naypyitaw. However, a senior military official told the Associated Press that Suu Kyi would be transferred to house arrest as part of the clemency measures, though this was not publicly confirmed in official statements. Under the amnesty terms, her sentence would be reduced by four and a half years, leaving approximately 22.5 years remaining.

The amnesty came one week after Min Aung Hlaing was inaugurated as president following an election widely condemned by critics as neither free nor fair and designed to entrench military rule. Among those also freed was filmmaker Shin Daewe, who had been sentenced to life imprisonment under counterterrorism laws. Since the 2021 coup, nearly 8,000 civilians have been killed and roughly 22,170 political detainees remain imprisoned.

stories.headline: Update: French Widow Marie-Thérèse Returns Home After ICE Detention, Arrives in Shock
stories.summary:
Marie-Thérèse, an 86-year-old French woman who was detained by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement in Alabama, has returned to France, French Foreign Minister Jean-Noël Barrot confirmed Friday morning. She was reunited with her children at Paris-Charles de Gaulle Airport, where witnesses said she was still wearing her prison uniform, which was covered in stains and holes.

Her son described her as being in a state of shock and physically and spiritually exhausted after the ordeal. Marie-Thérèse had moved to Alabama after marrying an American man she had reconnected with after decades apart; he died in January, leaving her immigration status uncertain as her green card application remained pending.

The U.S. Department of Homeland Security had maintained she was an illegal alien who overstayed her visa, while her family argued she was awaiting lawful permanent residency. The case drew significant international attention and diplomatic pressure from Paris, coming amid the Trump administration's broad expansion of ICE's enforcement activities.

stories.headline: Update: Singer D4vd Arrested on Suspicion of Murdering Teenage Girl Whose Remains Were Found in His Car
stories.summary:
Los Angeles police arrested 21-year-old singer D4vd, whose legal name is David Anthony Burke, on Thursday on suspicion of murdering 14-year-old Celeste Rivas Hernandez, whose decomposed remains were discovered in a cadaver bag inside a Tesla registered to his Texas address last September. Burke surrendered in Hollywood and was booked into jail without bail; the case is to be presented to the district attorney's office on Monday.

The Los Angeles County District Attorney's office confirmed it expects to receive a case presentation Monday and will determine whether sufficient evidence exists to file charges. Under California law, Burke must be charged or released by 5 p.m. Monday under the 48-hour detention rule, which excludes weekends and court holidays.

Burke's lawyers denied the allegations, stating that actual evidence would show he did not murder Hernandez and was not the cause of her death. They also noted that no indictment had been returned by a grand jury and no criminal complaint had been filed at the time of the arrest. No official cause of death has been announced for Hernandez, whose remains were found severely decomposed when discovered at a Hollywood tow yard.

stories.headline: Update: DR Congo Receives First Group of U.S. Third-Country Deportees
stories.summary:
The first group of migrants deported by the United States to the Democratic Republic of Congo — approximately 15 individuals reported to be mostly Colombian and Peruvian nationals — arrived at N'djili International Airport in Kinshasa in the early hours of Friday. The Congolese government confirmed their arrival under short-stay permits and stressed that the arrangement is temporary, with Washington funding their reception, support, and care.

The deportations are part of the Trump administration's broad use of third-country agreements to remove migrants to nations other than their home countries. The U.S. has previously sent deportees to Ghana, South Sudan, Eswatini, and other countries. A minority report from the Senate Foreign Relations Committee estimated the administration has likely spent more than $40 million on third-country deportations through January 2026.

The arrangement occurs alongside parallel U.S. negotiations with DR Congo over access to the country's significant reserves of cobalt, tantalum, lithium, and copper. The U.S. State Department declined to comment on the diplomatic communications but reaffirmed its commitment to ending illegal immigration. Kinshasa has described the scheme as consistent with international solidarity and human dignity, while emphasizing it does not represent a permanent relocation mechanism.

stories.headline: NBA Play-In: Suns Eliminate Warriors as Curry Held to 17; Magic Rout Hornets
stories.summary:
The Phoenix Suns eliminated the Golden State Warriors from the NBA play-in tournament Friday night, winning 111-96 to claim the Western Conference's No. 8 seed. Jalen Green led Phoenix with 36 points on 14-of-20 shooting, while Stephen Curry was held to 17 points on 4-of-16 shooting, ending the Warriors' season.

Both Devin Booker and Draymond Green were ejected in the fourth quarter after exchanging words and receiving double technical fouls. The Suns will face the defending NBA champion Oklahoma City Thunder beginning Sunday in the first round of the playoffs.

In the Eastern Conference play-in, the Orlando Magic routed the Charlotte Hornets to claim the No. 8 seed, with Paolo Banchero scoring 25 points and Orlando building a 35-point first-half lead. The Magic will face the Detroit Pistons starting Sunday. Charlotte's LaMelo Ball scored 21 of his 23 points in the third quarter but could not overcome the early deficit.
